_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 1
questions.prompt: Which block runs whether try succeeds or fails?
choices: finally
catch only
throw
return
questions.explanation: A finally block runs after the try and any matching catch, making it suitable for cleanup that both paths require.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 2
questions.prompt: What is wrong with an empty catch block?
choices: It hides the failure and its evidence
Catch requires a return value
It retries forever
It converts the error to text
questions.explanation: The program silently continues, so neither the user nor the developer can see that save failed or why it failed.

questions.title: Trace Error Handling
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: JSON.parse throws before the Loaded message, so catch provides a controlled response. The resulting output is 'Could not read that JSON'.

questions.title: Error Handling good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Error Handling?
choices: Protect only operations you expect can fail.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Protect only operations you expect can fail. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: try { riskyWork(); } catch (error) {}
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: An empty catch makes the program look successful even though its work failed. Handle an expected case or preserve the error for a layer that can.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 6
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

Do not wrap a whole application in one broad try block. Keep the protected section close to the operation whose failure you understand, so unrelated programming mistakes are not mislabeled as expected input problems.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Do not wrap a whole application in one broad try block. Keep the protected section close to the operation whose failure you understand, so unrelated programming mistakes are not mislabeled as expected input problems.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Error Handling?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Read the Error name and message.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Read the Error name and message. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Error Handling?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Give users a specific recovery path.
questions.explanation: Give users a specific recovery path. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Error Handling?
choices: Rethrow errors the current layer cannot handle.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Rethrow errors the current layer cannot handle. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: JSON.parse throws before the Loaded message, so catch provides a controlled response. The output is 'Could not read that JSON'.
